title: Carlsen vs Caruana — 2018 WCC, Game 2
description: In a Queen's Gambit Declined Caruana equalizes effortlessly with Black, neutralizing even Carlsen's provocative 15.Ne5?! to reach a calm 49-move draw. Annotated by GM Sam Shankland.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator calls 6...c5 ("!?") "the first interesting moment." Why?

options.text: It is a less common choice than Caruana's usual 6...Nbd7, a small surprise rather than the main path.
options.explanation: Correct. The note reads: "Caruana chooses a less common move than the typical Nbd7, which he has played a lot in the past with reasonable success," and the sideline calls 6...Nbd7 "much more common."

options.text: It is a brand-new novelty never before seen at any level.
options.explanation: The annotator only calls it less common than 6...Nbd7, not an unheard-of novelty.

options.text: It immediately wins White's c4-pawn by force.
options.explanation: No such claim is made; 6...c5 is a developing/structural choice, not a pawn grab.

conceptChecks.hint: Compare it to Caruana's usual move in this position.

conceptChecks.prompt: How does the annotator characterize Caruana's 10...Rd8 ("!?")?

options.text: An uncommon try — not a novelty, but he can't find any games by 2600+ players, unlike the normal 10...Re8.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Not a novelty, but definitely not a common move, and I can't find any games played by anyone over 2600," while 10...Re8 "is a much more normal move."

options.text: The established main line that nearly everyone plays here.
options.explanation: The opposite — the annotator says 10...Re8 is the normal move and that 10...Rd8 is rarely seen.

options.text: A clear blunder that loses a pawn.
options.explanation: The annotator never calls it a mistake; Black is "supposed to be doing all right" in this position.

conceptChecks.hint: Did the annotator find strong-player precedents for it?

conceptChecks.prompt: What is the point of 11...Ne4 ("!") according to the annotation?

options.text: Black takes the chance to compromise White's queenside pawn structure.
options.explanation: Correct. The note is exactly: "Black takes his chance to compromise White's queenside structure" — after ...Nxc3 bxc3 White's pawns are damaged.

options.text: It wins a piece by a hidden tactic.
options.explanation: No tactic is claimed; the point is the long-term structural damage to White's queenside.

options.text: It sets up an immediate mating attack on White's king.
options.explanation: The annotator describes a structural idea, not an attack on the king.

conceptChecks.hint: Look at what happens to White's pawns after the knights come off.

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator attach a "?!" to 15.Ne5?

options.text: Carlsen is "asking for trouble" — accurate play still holds, but the move needlessly invites difficulties.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Magnus is asking for trouble with this move, but if he plays accurately he is not going to be in any real danger."

options.text: It is a losing blunder that drops material by force.
options.explanation: No — the annotator says that with accurate play Carlsen "is not going to be in any real danger."

options.text: It wins a pawn and gives White a clear advantage.
options.explanation: The opposite — the move makes things harder for White, not better.

conceptChecks.hint: The note is about risk, not about an outright mistake.

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator prefer 19.Rxd8+ ("!") over the immediate 19.fxe5?

options.text: After 19.fxe5? Bd7! Black can claim an edge, so White inserts the rook trade first to keep the balance.
options.explanation: Correct. The sideline reads: "19.fxe5? Is less accurate and Black can claim an edge with 19...Bd7! 20.Bxb7 Rab8!"
The annotator only calls 19.fxe5 "less accurate," giving Black an edge — not a losing move.
No material is won; it is simply the more precise move order to hold.

conceptChecks.hint: What does Black get to play if White recaptures on e5 first?

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does Carlsen play 28.a6 ("!") in the rook ending?

options.text: He forces matters at once to liquidate the queenside before Black's king can reach c6, securing the draw.
options.explanation: Correct. The note warns the ending is "a little dangerous for White if Black can get the king to c6," so "Magnus forced matters right away" with 28.a6 and 29.c5.

options.text: He is pressing for a win with an outside passed pawn.
options.explanation: White is defending and steering toward a draw, not playing for a win.

options.text: It sets up a mating net against the black king.
options.explanation: There is no mating idea; the point is to simplify the queenside into a dead draw.

conceptChecks.hint: Where did the annotator say Black's king must not be allowed to go?
